name: Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics
description: General principles for interaction design by Jakob Nielsen

heuristics.name: Visibility of System Status
heuristics.description: The design should always keep users informed about what is going on through appropriate feedback within a reasonable amount of time
bad_examples: Buttons that don't show loading state
implementation_tips: Use skeleton screens for content loading
Provide progress percentages for long operations
Confirm successful actions with visual feedback

heuristics.name: Match Between System and Real World
heuristics.description: The design should speak the users' language, using familiar words, phrases, and concepts
examples: Calendar widgets show weeks starting on cultural norm
Icons match real-world metaphors (envelope for email)

heuristics.name: User Control and Freedom
heuristics.description: Users often perform actions by mistake and need a clearly marked 'emergency exit'
examples: Cancel buttons on forms and modals
bad_examples: No way to undo destructive actions
Forced multi-step flows with no exit
implementation_tips: Allow users to exit wizards mid-flow

heuristics.name: Consistency and Standards
heuristics.description: Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing
examples: Same icon represents same action throughout
Uniform color scheme for statuses (green=success, red=error)
bad_examples: Save button in different locations on different pages
implementation_tips: Follow platform guidelines (iOS HIG, Material Design)

heuristics.name: Error Prevention
heuristics.description: Good designs prevent problems from occurring in the first place
examples: Disabled submit button until form is valid
bad_examples: Easy to accidentally delete important data
implementation_tips: Implement 'Are you sure?' for destructive actions

heuristics.name: Recognition Rather than Recall
heuristics.description: Minimize memory load by making elements, actions, and options visible
examples: Dropdown menus instead of command line
Visual previews instead of file names
bad_examples: Complex keyboard shortcuts with no hints

heuristics.name: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use
heuristics.description: Provide accelerators for expert users while remaining accessible to novices
bad_examples: Must always go through multi-step wizard
implementation_tips: Allow skipping optional steps in wizards
Provide both simple and advanced modes

heuristics.name: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design
heuristics.description: Interfaces should not contain information that is irrelevant or rarely needed
bad_examples: Cluttered interfaces with every feature visible
Too many options on one screen
implementation_tips: Show only what's needed for current task
Use progressive disclosure for advanced features

heuristics.name: Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors
heuristics.description: Error messages should be expressed in plain language, precisely indicate the problem, and suggest a solution
examples: Specific error messages: 'Password must contain at least 8 characters'
bad_examples: Errors that don't say how to fix
implementation_tips: Use plain language (no error codes alone)
Explain what went wrong and why
Keep user's input when showing errors

heuristics.name: Help and Documentation
heuristics.description: Provide help and documentation that is easy to search and focused on the user's task
bad_examples: Help text hidden or hard to find
Generic help that doesn't address specific features
implementation_tips: Use placeholder text to show format

evaluation_questions: Does the user always know what's happening? (Visibility)
Is the language familiar to users? (Match Real World)
Can users easily undo actions? (User Control)
Are errors prevented before they occur? (Error Prevention)
Are options visible vs requiring memorization? (Recognition)
Are there shortcuts for power users? (Flexibility)
Is the design clean and focused? (Minimalist)
Are error messages helpful and actionable? (Error Recovery)
Is help available when needed? (Help & Documentation)
